name: Chicken Katsu Curry
description: Crispy panko-breaded chicken cutlet served over rice with a rich Japanese curry sauce.
instructions: Dice onion, carrot, and potato into bite-sized pieces and simmer in 600 ml water until tender, about 15 minutes.
Turn off heat, add curry roux blocks, and stir until dissolved; simmer on low 5 minutes until thickened.
Season chicken breasts with salt, dredge in flour, dip in beaten egg, then coat generously with panko.
Heat oil in a deep pan to 170 C and fry chicken 5-6 minutes per side until golden and cooked through.
Rest chicken on a wire rack for 2 minutes, then slice into strips.
Serve sliced katsu over steamed rice with curry sauce ladled alongside.
tips: Press panko firmly onto chicken for a crunchier crust.
Japanese curry roux brands like Golden Curry or Vermont Curry are widely available.

name: Beef Gyudon (Rice Bowl)
description: Thinly sliced beef and onions simmered in a sweet soy-dashi broth, served over steamed rice.
instructions: Slice onion into thin half-moons and separate the layers.
Combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and sake in a shallow pan and bring to a simmer.
Add onion slices and cook for 5 minutes until softened.
Spread beef slices over the onions and simmer 3-4 minutes until just cooked through.
Skim any foam, then spoon beef and sauce over bowls of steamed rice.
Top with sliced spring onion and pickled ginger.
tips: Use beef sliced paper-thin (shabu-shabu cut) for the most authentic texture.
A raw egg yolk dropped on top is a popular Yoshinoya-style addition.

name: Tonkatsu (Pork Cutlet)
description: Deep-fried panko-crusted pork loin served with shredded cabbage and tonkatsu sauce.
instructions: Score pork edges to prevent curling, season with salt and pepper.
Set up breading station: flour, beaten egg, and panko in separate shallow dishes.
Dredge pork in flour, dip in egg, then press firmly into panko on both sides.
Heat oil to 170 C in a deep pot and fry pork for 6-7 minutes, flipping once, until deep golden.
Rest on a wire rack for 3 minutes, then slice into 2 cm strips.
Serve over rice with finely shredded cabbage, tonkatsu sauce, and a lemon wedge.
tips: Double-fry for extra crunch: fry 4 minutes, rest 2, then fry again for 2 minutes.
Make tonkatsu sauce by mixing Worcestershire sauce, ketchup, and a pinch of sugar.

name: Yakitori (Grilled Chicken Skewers)
description: Bite-sized chicken pieces grilled on bamboo skewers with a sweet soy tare glaze.
instructions: Cut chicken thighs into 2.5 cm cubes and cut spring onion whites into 3 cm pieces.
Soak bamboo skewers in water for 20 minutes to prevent burning.
Make tare sauce by simmering soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar until slightly thickened, about 5 minutes.
Thread chicken and spring onion pieces alternately onto skewers.
Grill skewers over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side, brushing with tare sauce twice during cooking.
Serve with a final brush of tare and a sprinkle of shichimi togarashi.
tips: Chicken thighs stay juicier than breast on the grill.
For authentic yakitori, grill over binchotan charcoal if available.

name: Saba Shioyaki (Grilled Mackerel)
description: Salt-grilled mackerel fillet with crispy skin, a staple of Japanese home cooking.
instructions: Pat mackerel fillets dry and sprinkle both sides generously with sea salt.
Let salted fish rest for 20 minutes, then pat dry again to remove moisture.
Grill skin-side up under a broiler on high for 5-6 minutes until skin is blistered and crispy.
Flip and grill flesh-side for 2-3 minutes until just cooked through.
Grate daikon radish finely for a fresh condiment.
Serve mackerel with grated daikon, lemon wedge, steamed rice, and miso soup.
tips: The salt-rest step draws out moisture and concentrates flavor — do not skip it.
Score the skin lightly if fillets are thick to ensure even cooking.

name: Nikujaga (Meat and Potato Stew)
description: Hearty Japanese comfort stew of thinly sliced beef, potatoes, and onions in a sweet soy broth.
instructions: Cut potatoes into large chunks, slice onion into wedges, and cut carrot into rolling cuts.
Heat oil in a pot, stir-fry beef until just browned, then add onion and cook 2 minutes.
Add potatoes, carrot, and drained shirataki noodles; stir briefly.
Pour in dashi, sake, sugar, soy sauce, and mirin; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, cover with a drop lid (otoshibuta), and simmer 20-25 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Let rest 10 minutes off heat for flavors to absorb before serving.
tips: A drop lid (or circle of parchment paper) keeps ingredients submerged for even flavor.
Nikujaga tastes even better reheated the next day.

name: Oyakodon (Chicken and Egg Rice Bowl)
description: Chicken and onion simmered in sweet dashi broth, finished with softly set eggs over rice.
instructions: Cut chicken thigh into bite-sized pieces and slice onion into thin half-moons.
Combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a small lidded skillet and bring to a simmer.
Add onion and simmer 3 minutes until softened, then add chicken pieces.
Cook chicken for 5 minutes until just done, keeping the broth at a gentle simmer.
Beat eggs lightly (don't over-mix), pour over chicken in a spiral, cover, and cook 30 seconds.
Slide the egg-chicken mixture over bowls of hot rice and garnish with mitsuba.
tips: For silky eggs, pour in two stages: first half to set, then the second half for a runny finish.
Use a small pan that fits over one rice bowl for the best shape.

name: Teriyaki Salmon
description: Pan-seared salmon glazed with a homemade teriyaki sauce of soy, mirin, and sake.
instructions: Pat salmon fillets dry and season lightly with salt.
Mix soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar to make teriyaki sauce.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high and sear salmon skin-side down for 4 minutes.
Flip salmon and cook 2 more minutes, then pour teriyaki sauce into the pan.
Reduce sauce for 2 minutes, spooning it over the salmon until glossy and thickened.
Serve over rice, drizzle remaining sauce, and top with sesame seeds and sliced spring onion.
tips: Start skin-side down for crispy skin that holds the glaze beautifully.
Do not overcook salmon — it should be slightly translucent in the center.

name: Miso Glazed Eggplant (Nasu Dengaku)
description: Roasted eggplant halves topped with a sweet white miso glaze, a classic Japanese vegetarian dish.
instructions: Halve eggplants lengthwise and score the flesh in a crosshatch pattern.
Brush cut sides with vegetable oil and roast face-down at 200 C for 15 minutes until soft.
Meanwhile, whisk together miso paste, mirin, sugar, sake, and sesame oil in a small saucepan.
Heat miso glaze over low heat, stirring until smooth and slightly thickened, about 3 minutes.
Flip roasted eggplants, spread miso glaze generously over the cut sides.
Broil for 3-4 minutes until glaze is bubbly and lightly caramelized; top with sesame seeds and spring onion.
tips: Japanese eggplants are ideal due to their thin skin and creamy flesh.
The miso glaze can be made ahead and stored refrigerated for a week.

name: Vegetable Tempura
description: Lightly battered and fried seasonal vegetables with a crispy, airy coating served with tentsuyu dipping sauce.
instructions: Cut sweet potato into 5 mm rounds, eggplant into fan shapes, and trim green beans.
Make tentsuyu by simmering dashi, soy sauce, and mirin together; set aside.
Prepare batter by lightly mixing egg, ice water, flour, and cornstarch — leave lumps for crispiness.
Heat oil to 170 C; dip vegetables in batter and fry in small batches for 2-3 minutes until pale golden.
Drain on a wire rack and serve immediately while still crispy.
Serve with warm tentsuyu dipping sauce and grated daikon.
tips: Keep batter ice cold and lumpy — overmixing creates a heavy, doughy coating.
Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature.

name: Cold Soba Noodles (Zaru Soba)
description: Chilled buckwheat noodles served on a bamboo mat with concentrated tsuyu dipping sauce.
instructions: Boil soba noodles in plenty of unsalted water according to package directions, about 4-5 minutes.
Drain and rinse thoroughly under cold running water, rubbing gently to remove starch.
Make tsuyu by combining dashi, soy sauce, and mirin; chill in the refrigerator.
Arrange cold soba on a zaru (bamboo mat) or plate.
Finely slice spring onion, cut nori into thin strips, and prepare a small mound of wasabi.
Serve noodles with chilled tsuyu in a small cup, condiments on the side for mixing in.
tips: Rinse soba thoroughly — the starch makes them gummy if left on.
For 100% buckwheat soba (juwari), cook more gently as they break easily.

name: Udon Noodle Soup
description: Thick wheat noodles in a clear dashi-soy broth topped with kamaboko, spring onion, and tempura flakes.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a simmer and season with soy sauce, mirin, and salt.
Cook udon noodles according to package directions, then drain and rinse briefly.
Divide hot noodles between two deep bowls.
Ladle the hot broth over the noodles until just covered.
Top with sliced kamaboko, chopped spring onion, and tempura flakes.
Serve immediately with shichimi togarashi on the side.
tips: Fresh or frozen udon has a much better chewy texture than dried.
For kitsune udon, top with sweet simmered aburaage (fried tofu pouch).

name: Japanese Curry Rice
description: Mild, thick, and sweet Japanese-style curry with chunks of potato, carrot, and onion over rice.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces, dice potatoes and carrots, and slice onions.
Heat oil in a large pot, cook onions until translucent, then add chicken and brown lightly.
Add carrots and potatoes, stir briefly, then pour in water and bring to a boil.
Skim foam, reduce heat, and simmer covered for 15 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Turn off heat, add curry roux blocks, and stir until completely dissolved.
Simmer on low for 5-10 minutes until thick, then serve over steamed rice with fukujinzuke.
tips: Mixing two different curry roux brands creates a more complex flavor.
Japanese curry thickens as it cools — reheat with a splash of water.

name: Agedashi Tofu
description: Lightly fried silken tofu cubes in a warm dashi-soy broth with grated ginger and daikon.
instructions: Drain tofu and cut into 3 cm cubes; pat thoroughly dry with paper towels.
Dust tofu cubes lightly with cornstarch, shaking off excess.
Heat oil to 180 C and fry tofu cubes for 2-3 minutes until golden and crisp outside.
Drain on a wire rack and place 4-5 pieces in each serving bowl.
Heat dashi, soy sauce, and mirin together for the sauce; pour over fried tofu.
Top with grated daikon, grated ginger, sliced spring onion, and bonito flakes.
tips: Pressing tofu removes moisture and prevents oil splatter during frying.
Serve immediately — the crispy coating softens quickly in the broth.

name: Chawanmushi (Steamed Egg Custard)
description: Silky savory egg custard steamed with shrimp, chicken, and mushroom hidden inside.
instructions: Beat eggs gently (avoid bubbles), mix with cooled dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and salt.
Strain egg mixture through a fine sieve for a silky texture.
Place shrimp, diced chicken, sliced shiitake, and kamaboko into chawanmushi cups.
Pour egg mixture gently into cups, filling to 80% capacity.
Steam on low heat for 12-15 minutes until custard is set but still jiggly.
Top with mitsuba sprigs and serve warm.
tips: Low, gentle steam is key — high heat creates bubbles and a pockmarked texture.
Cover cups with foil to prevent condensation dripping into the custard.

name: Takikomi Gohan (Mixed Rice)
description: Japanese seasoned rice cooked with mushrooms, vegetables, and chicken in dashi, soy, and sake.
instructions: Wash rice and soak for 30 minutes, then drain.
Cut chicken into small pieces; slice shiitake, carrot into matchsticks, and shave gobo thinly.
Slice aburaage into thin strips and briefly blanch to remove excess oil.
Place rice in a rice cooker, add dashi, soy sauce, sake, mirin, and salt.
Arrange all toppings over the rice without stirring.
Cook using the regular rice cooker setting; once done, fold toppings gently into the rice.
tips: Do not stir before cooking — toppings sit on top so the rice cooks evenly underneath.
Gobo adds an earthy depth; soak shavings in vinegar water to prevent browning.

name: Niku Udon (Meat Udon)
description: Chewy udon noodles in dashi broth topped with sweet soy-simmered sliced beef and spring onion.
instructions: Simmer soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and sake in a small pan; add sliced onion and cook until soft.
Add beef slices to the sweet soy mixture and cook until just done, about 3 minutes; set aside.
Bring dashi stock to a simmer and season with 1 tbsp soy sauce.
Cook udon noodles per package, drain, and divide into bowls.
Ladle hot broth over noodles, then top with the simmered beef and onion.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and shichimi togarashi.
tips: Keep beef slightly rare when simmering — it will continue cooking in the hot broth.
Frozen udon noodles give the best chewy texture.

name: Karaage (Japanese Fried Chicken)
description: Double-fried bite-sized chicken thigh marinated in soy, ginger, and garlic with a light potato starch crust.
instructions: Cut chicken thighs into 3 cm pieces; combine with soy sauce, sake, grated ginger, and minced garlic.
Marinate for 30 minutes (or up to overnight in the fridge).
Coat marinated chicken pieces in potato starch, shaking off excess.
Heat oil to 160 C and fry chicken for 3 minutes; remove and rest for 3 minutes.
Increase oil to 180 C and fry again for 2 minutes until dark golden and extra crispy.
Serve with lemon wedges and Japanese mayonnaise for dipping.
tips: The double-fry method is essential for the signature extra-crispy karaage texture.
Potato starch gives a lighter, crispier crust than regular flour.

name: Hayashi Rice (Hashed Beef Rice)
description: Tender sliced beef and mushrooms in a rich demi-glace sauce served over steamed rice.
instructions: Slice onions thinly and mushrooms into quarters.
Melt butter in a large pan, cook onions on medium-low for 15 minutes until deeply caramelized.
Add beef slices and mushrooms, cook until beef is browned, about 3 minutes.
Pour in red wine and simmer until reduced by half.
Add demi-glace, tomato paste, ketchup, and Worcestershire sauce; simmer for 15 minutes.
Serve over steamed rice and garnish with fresh parsley.
tips: Patiently caramelizing the onions is the secret to the sauce's depth.
Hayashi roux cubes are a shortcut available at Japanese grocery stores.

name: Chirashi Sushi
description: Seasoned sushi rice topped with an assortment of sashimi-grade fish, egg, and vegetables.
instructions: Cook sushi rice, then fold in a mixture of rice vinegar, sugar, and salt while still hot; fan to cool.
Make thin egg crepe (kinshi tamago): beat eggs, cook as a thin omelet, and slice into thin strips.
Boil shrimp for 2 minutes, peel, devein, and butterfly.
Slice salmon and tuna into thin pieces at an angle.
Spread seasoned rice in a wide bowl and arrange fish, shrimp, egg strips, and sliced cucumber on top.
Garnish with shredded nori, pickled ginger, and serve with soy sauce on the side.
tips: Fan the rice as you fold in vinegar for a glossy, non-sticky finish.
Use the freshest fish possible — quality of sashimi makes or breaks this dish.

name: Tofu Steak with Mushroom Sauce
description: Pan-seared firm tofu steaks with a savory mushroom and butter soy sauce.
instructions: Drain tofu, press for 15 minutes, then cut into 2 cm thick steaks.
Dust tofu steaks with cornstarch on all sides.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan and sear tofu for 3-4 minutes per side until golden; set aside.
In the same pan, melt butter and saute minced garlic and shimeji mushrooms for 3 minutes.
Add soy sauce, mirin, and pepper; cook until sauce is slightly reduced.
Pour mushroom sauce over tofu steaks and garnish with sliced spring onion.
tips: Press tofu well for a firm texture that sears properly without crumbling.
Shimeji, enoki, or shiitake all work well for the sauce.

name: Kinpira Gobo (Braised Burdock Root)
description: Matchstick-cut burdock root and carrot stir-fried in a sweet soy glaze with sesame and chili.
instructions: Scrub gobo and shave into thin matchsticks; soak in vinegar water for 5 minutes to prevent browning.
Cut carrot into similar-sized matchsticks.
Heat sesame oil in a pan over medium heat and stir-fry gobo for 3 minutes.
Add carrot and red chili flakes, stir-fry another 2 minutes.
Add soy sauce, mirin, and sugar; cook stirring until liquid is absorbed, about 3 minutes.
Sprinkle with sesame seeds and serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Kinpira keeps well for 4-5 days refrigerated — perfect make-ahead side.
The shaving technique for gobo is called sasagaki — scrape like sharpening a pencil.

name: Hijiki Seaweed Salad
description: Reconstituted hijiki seaweed simmered with soy sauce, carrots, and aburaage in a sweet dashi broth.
instructions: Soak dried hijiki in water for 30 minutes until fully reconstituted, then drain.
Cut carrot into thin matchsticks and slice aburaage into thin strips.
Heat sesame oil in a pan and stir-fry hijiki and carrot for 2 minutes.
Add aburaage strips and stir briefly.
Pour in dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar; simmer on low for 10 minutes until liquid is mostly absorbed.
Toss in cooked edamame and serve warm or chilled.
tips: Hijiki expands dramatically when soaked — 30 g dry yields about 150 g reconstituted.
This dish is a staple in Japanese bento boxes and keeps well for 3-4 days.

name: Japanese Hamburg Steak
description: Juicy Japanese-style hamburger patties with a tangy demi-glace and ketchup sauce.
instructions: Finely dice onion, saute in butter until translucent, and cool completely.
Soak panko in milk; combine with ground meat, cooled onion, egg, salt, and nutmeg.
Knead mixture well, then form into 2 oval patties, tossing between hands to remove air pockets.
Heat oil in a skillet, sear patties on high for 2 minutes per side until browned.
Add 50 ml water, cover, and steam-cook on medium-low for 8 minutes until cooked through.
Remove patties; add ketchup and Worcestershire to the pan drippings, simmer into a sauce, and pour over.
tips: The toss-between-hands technique removes air and prevents cracking during cooking.
A mix of beef and pork gives the best juicy texture.

name: Kakuni (Braised Pork Belly)
description: Melt-in-your-mouth pork belly slowly braised in soy sauce, sake, and ginger until tender.
instructions: Cut pork belly into 4 cm cubes.
Boil pork cubes with spring onion greens and ginger slices for 20 minutes to remove excess fat; drain and discard water.
Place par-boiled pork in a clean pot with fresh water, sake, soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and fresh ginger slices.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest simmer, cover with a drop lid, and cook for 90 minutes.
Check tenderness — pork should break apart easily; if not, simmer another 20 minutes.
Serve pork with reduced braising liquid and a dab of karashi mustard.
tips: The initial boil removes impurities and excess fat for a cleaner final braise.
Low and slow is key — rushing the braise results in tough pork.

name: Shogayaki (Ginger Pork)
description: Thin pork slices pan-fried with a bold ginger-soy sauce, a beloved Japanese lunch set staple.
instructions: Grate ginger finely and mix with soy sauce, mirin, and sake to make the sauce.
Lightly dust pork slices with flour.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium-high and cook pork for 2 minutes per side until golden.
Pour ginger sauce over the pork and cook for 1-2 minutes until sauce is glossy and caramelized.
Pile shredded raw cabbage on serving plates.
Arrange ginger pork alongside cabbage with steamed rice.
tips: Fresh grated ginger is essential — jarred ginger lacks the punch.
Shogayaki is the most popular teishoku (lunch set) dish in Japan.

name: Miso Soup with Tofu
description: Classic Japanese miso soup with silken tofu, wakame seaweed, and spring onion in dashi broth.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a gentle simmer in a pot.
Rehydrate dried wakame in water for 5 minutes, then drain.
Cut silken tofu into 1.5 cm cubes.
Place miso paste in a ladle, submerge in the broth, and dissolve by stirring with chopsticks.
Add tofu cubes and wakame to the soup; heat through for 1 minute without boiling.
Serve in bowls topped with finely sliced spring onion.
tips: Never boil miso soup after adding the paste — boiling destroys the flavor and probiotics.
A combination of white and red miso gives a more complex taste.

name: Mapo Tofu (Japanese-style)
description: Mild Japanese adaptation of mapo tofu with silken tofu in a savory miso-soy meat sauce.
instructions: Cut tofu into 2 cm cubes and gently warm in salted water for 3 minutes; drain.
Heat sesame oil in a wok, stir-fry minced garlic and ginger for 30 seconds.
Add ground pork and cook until browned, breaking into fine crumbles.
Stir in doubanjiang, miso paste, soy sauce, and mirin with 150 ml water; simmer 3 minutes.
Gently slide in tofu cubes, stir carefully, and simmer 3 minutes.
Thicken with cornstarch slurry, garnish with spring onion, and serve over rice.
tips: Japanese-style uses less chili than Sichuan — adjust doubanjiang to taste.
Warming tofu in salted water firms it slightly so it doesn't break apart.

name: Yaki Onigiri (Grilled Rice Balls)
description: Crispy-surfaced grilled rice balls brushed with soy sauce, a popular Japanese snack or side.
instructions: Cook rice and let it cool slightly until handleable but still warm.
Wet hands with salted water and shape rice firmly into triangular onigiri.
Mix soy sauce and mirin to make the glaze.
Heat a non-stick pan or grill over medium heat and brush lightly with sesame oil.
Place onigiri on the pan and grill for 3-4 minutes per side until a golden crust forms.
Brush soy-mirin glaze on each side during the last minute of grilling; sprinkle with sesame seeds.
tips: Pack the rice tightly — loose onigiri will fall apart on the grill.
A cast iron pan gives the best crust if you don't have a charcoal grill.

name: Bibimbap
description: Korean mixed rice bowl with seasoned vegetables, gochujang sauce, and a fried egg on top.
instructions: Blanch spinach and bean sprouts separately; squeeze dry and season each with sesame oil, salt, and garlic.
Julienne carrot and zucchini; saute each separately with a pinch of salt until just tender.
Slice shiitake mushrooms and saute with soy sauce until golden.
Fry eggs sunny-side up with runny yolks.
Arrange rice in bowls, place each vegetable in separate sections around the bowl.
Top with fried egg, a generous dollop of gochujang, and sesame seeds; mix before eating.
tips: For dolsot bibimbap, heat a stone bowl until smoking and press rice in for a crispy crust.
Each vegetable should be seasoned and cooked separately for distinct flavors.

name: Bulgogi (Korean BBQ Beef)
description: Thinly sliced beef marinated in a sweet soy-pear sauce, grilled or pan-fried until caramelized.
instructions: Slice beef thinly against the grain (freezing for 30 minutes makes slicing easier).
Blend Asian pear, garlic, and ginger into a puree for the marinade base.
Combine puree with soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and pepper; marinate beef for at least 1 hour.
Slice onion into rings and cut spring onion into 5 cm pieces.
Grill or pan-fry beef and onion over high heat for 2-3 minutes per side until caramelized.
Garnish with spring onion and sesame seeds; serve with rice and lettuce wraps.
tips: Asian pear is a natural meat tenderizer — do not skip it.
Do not overcrowd the pan or the beef will steam rather than caramelize.

name: Japchae (Glass Noodle Stir-Fry)
description: Sweet potato glass noodles stir-fried with colorful vegetables, beef, and a sweet soy-sesame sauce.
instructions: Cook dangmyeon noodles per package, drain, and cut into shorter lengths with scissors.
Slice beef thinly, marinate in 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tsp sugar, and minced garlic.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, and season with sesame oil and salt.
Julienne carrot, bell pepper, and onion; slice mushrooms; saute each separately.
Stir-fry marinated beef until cooked through.
Combine everything in a large bowl with remaining soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil; toss well and top with sesame seeds.
tips: Cooking each vegetable separately preserves its color and texture — essential for authentic japchae.
Japchae is served at room temperature and tastes great the next day.

name: Kimchi Jjigae (Kimchi Stew)
description: Spicy, sour stew of fermented kimchi with pork belly and tofu simmered until deeply flavored.
instructions: Cut pork belly into bite-sized slices and kimchi into large pieces.
Heat sesame oil in a pot and saute pork belly until fat renders, about 3 minutes.
Add kimchi and stir-fry together for 2-3 minutes until fragrant.
Add water, gochugaru, soy sauce, and minced garlic; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 15 minutes to develop deep flavor.
Add cubed tofu and sliced onion, simmer 5 more minutes, and garnish with spring onion.
tips: Use aged (sour) kimchi for the best jjigae — fresh kimchi lacks the depth.
Kimchi jjigae tastes better each time it is reheated.

name: Sundubu Jjigae (Soft Tofu Stew)
description: Bubbling-hot spicy stew with uncurdled soft tofu, seafood, and a raw egg cracked on top.
instructions: Heat sesame oil in a stone pot (ttukbaegi) and saute minced garlic and gochugaru for 30 seconds.
Add diced onion and stir briefly, then pour in anchovy-kelp stock.
Add clams and shrimp, bring to a boil, and cook 3 minutes until clams open.
Squeeze in soft tofu in large spoonfuls, add gochujang, and stir gently.
Simmer vigorously for 5 minutes until bubbling fiercely.
Crack a raw egg on top, garnish with spring onion, and serve immediately while still bubbling.
tips: A stone pot (ttukbaegi) retains heat and keeps the stew bubbling at the table.
The egg cooks from the residual heat — serve immediately for the best texture.

name: Dakgalbi (Spicy Chicken Stir-Fry)
description: Chunky chicken thighs stir-fried with cabbage, sweet potato, and rice cakes in a gochujang sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces and chop cabbage into large squares.
Slice sweet potato into thin half-moons and soak rice cakes in warm water.
Make sauce by mixing gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, minced garlic, ginger, and sesame oil.
Toss chicken in sauce and marinate for 15 minutes.
Heat a large flat pan, add marinated chicken, sweet potato, cabbage, and rice cakes.
Stir-fry over high heat for 10-12 minutes, turning frequently, until chicken is cooked and vegetables are tender.
tips: Add mozzarella cheese in the center for the popular cheese dakgalbi variation.
Dakgalbi is traditionally cooked at the table on a large iron plate.

name: Galbi Jjim (Braised Short Ribs)
description: Soy-braised beef short ribs with vegetables, slowly cooked until fall-off-the-bone tender.
instructions: Soak short ribs in cold water for 2 hours to draw out blood, then drain and blanch in boiling water for 5 minutes.
Blend Asian pear, garlic, and ginger into a puree.
Combine puree with soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil for the braising sauce.
Place blanched ribs in a pot, pour sauce over, add 500 ml water, and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and braise covered for 90 minutes until meat is very tender.
Add large-cut carrot, daikon, jujubes, and chestnuts; cook 20 more minutes until vegetables are done.
tips: Soaking ribs overnight removes excess blood for a cleaner-tasting braise.
Galbi jjim is a celebratory dish served at Korean holidays and special occasions.

name: Korean Fried Chicken
description: Double-fried ultra-crispy chicken coated in a sticky sweet-spicy gochujang glaze.
instructions: Pat chicken wings dry, season with salt and pepper, then toss in a mix of cornstarch and flour.
Heat oil to 160 C, fry wings in batches for 8 minutes until cooked through; drain and rest 5 minutes.
Increase oil to 180 C and double-fry wings for 3-4 minutes until shatteringly crisp.
Make sauce: combine gochujang, honey, soy sauce, rice vinegar, minced garlic, and ginger in a pan; heat until bubbling.
Toss crispy fried chicken in the sauce until evenly coated.
Sprinkle with sesame seeds and serve immediately.
tips: Double frying is the secret to Korean fried chicken's signature crunch that stays crispy even with sauce.
For yangnyeom chicken, use this spicy glaze; for dakgangjeong, use a sweeter soy garlic version.

name: Tteokbokki (Spicy Rice Cakes)
description: Chewy rice cakes simmered in a fiery gochujang sauce with fish cakes, a beloved Korean street food.
instructions: Soak rice cakes in warm water for 10 minutes if using dried; separate any stuck pieces.
Cut fish cake sheets into triangles or bite-sized pieces.
Bring anchovy-kelp stock to a boil and stir in gochujang, gochugaru, sugar, and soy sauce.
Add rice cakes and simmer for 8-10 minutes, stirring often, until the sauce thickens and rice cakes are chewy.
Add fish cake pieces and halved boiled eggs in the last 3 minutes.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and serve piping hot.
tips: Stir frequently — rice cakes stick easily and the sauce thickens fast.
Add ramen noodles for the popular rabokki variation.

name: Jeyuk Bokkeum (Spicy Pork Stir-Fry)
description: Thinly sliced pork stir-fried in a fiery gochujang and gochugaru sauce with onions and cabbage.
instructions: Slice pork thinly and combine with gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and ginger; marinate 15 minutes.
Slice onion into half-moons, cut cabbage into squares, and chop spring onion.
Heat sesame oil in a large pan or wok over high heat.
Add marinated pork and stir-fry for 4-5 minutes until pork is cooked and edges are caramelized.
Add onion and cabbage, stir-fry 3 more minutes until vegetables are wilted but still crunchy.
Finish with spring onion and sesame seeds; serve with steamed rice.
tips: Pork shoulder has ideal fat content for this dish — too lean and it dries out.
Jeyuk bokkeum is a staple at Korean university cafeterias and home kitchens alike.

name: Samgyeopsal (Grilled Pork Belly)
description: Thick-cut pork belly slices grilled at the table, wrapped in lettuce with garlic, ssamjang, and kimchi.
instructions: Slice pork belly into 1 cm thick pieces.
Heat a grill or cast iron pan over high heat until very hot.
Grill pork belly slices for 3-4 minutes per side until cooked through and edges are crispy.
Slice raw garlic and cut green chilies into rings.
Cut grilled pork into bite-sized pieces with kitchen scissors at the table.
Wrap pork in lettuce and perilla leaves with a dab of ssamjang, slice of garlic, and kimchi.
tips: A meat-fat ratio of 3:7 to 4:6 is ideal for samgyeopsal.
Dip grilled pork in sesame oil mixed with salt for a simple classic accompaniment.

name: Doenjang Jjigae (Soybean Paste Stew)
description: Earthy, savory stew of fermented soybean paste with tofu, zucchini, and mushrooms.
instructions: Bring anchovy-kelp stock to a boil in a stone pot or heavy saucepan.
Dissolve doenjang paste into the stock, stirring well.
Add diced potato and sliced onion; simmer 5 minutes until potato softens.
Add cubed tofu, sliced zucchini, mushrooms, and minced garlic.
Simmer for 10 minutes on medium heat until all vegetables are tender.
Add sliced green chili and spring onion; serve bubbling hot.
tips: A small spoon of gochugaru adds a gentle heat if desired.
Doenjang jjigae is the most common everyday stew in Korean households.

name: Gamjatang (Pork Bone Soup)
description: Hearty spicy soup with pork neck bones, potatoes, and perilla seeds in a rich bone broth.
instructions: Soak pork neck bones in cold water for 2 hours to remove blood, then blanch in boiling water for 10 minutes; drain.
Place bones in a large pot with fresh water, ginger, and spring onion stems; boil for 60 minutes until meat is tender.
Remove bones temporarily; stir doenjang, gochugaru, garlic, and perilla seed powder into the broth.
Halve potatoes and add to the pot; simmer 15 minutes until nearly tender.
Return pork bones and add torn napa cabbage leaves; cook 5 more minutes.
Serve piping hot garnished with spring onion and sesame seeds.
tips: Perilla seed powder gives gamjatang its distinctive nutty, herbal flavor.
The meat should be so tender it falls off the bone when prodded.

name: Kongnamul Bap (Soybean Sprout Rice)
description: Rice cooked with soybean sprouts and seasoned with a spicy soy scallion sauce, a comforting one-pot dish.
instructions: Wash rice and soak for 30 minutes; drain.
Place rice in a heavy pot with the standard amount of water.
Season ground pork with 1 tbsp soy sauce and spread over the rice.
Top with washed and trimmed soybean sprouts — do not stir.
Cook covered on medium heat until boiling, then reduce to lowest heat for 15 minutes.
Make sauce by mixing remaining soy sauce, gochugaru, sesame oil, garlic, spring onion, and sesame seeds; stir into the finished rice.
tips: Do not open the lid during cooking — steam must stay trapped for fluffy rice and tender sprouts.
The crispy rice at the bottom (nurungji) is a prized part of this dish.

name: Haemul Pajeon (Seafood Pancake)
description: Crispy Korean pancake loaded with scallions, shrimp, and squid, served with a soy dipping sauce.
instructions: Cut spring onions into lengths matching the diameter of your pan; clean and slice shrimp and squid.
Mix flour, cornstarch, salt, egg, and ice water into a thin batter — leave some lumps.
Heat a generous amount of oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat.
Lay spring onions in the pan in a single layer, then scatter seafood over them.
Pour batter evenly over the scallions and seafood; press gently with a spatula.
Fry for 4-5 minutes until bottom is golden and crispy, flip, and fry 3-4 more minutes; serve with soy-vinegar dipping sauce.
tips: Press the pancake flat while cooking for maximum crispiness.
Serve immediately — pajeon loses its crunch quickly.

name: Dakdoritang (Spicy Chicken Stew)
description: Bone-in chicken pieces braised with potatoes, carrots, and onions in a spicy gochugaru-gochujang sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into large pieces and score the thick parts for even cooking.
Quarter potatoes, cut carrots into thick rounds, and cut onion into large wedges.
Mix gochugaru, gochujang, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and sugar into a sauce.
Place chicken in a pot, pour sauce over, add water, and bring to a boil.
Add potatoes, carrots, and onion; cover and simmer for 25-30 minutes until chicken is cooked and potatoes are tender.
Garnish with spring onion and serve with steamed rice.
tips: The sauce should be thick and coat the chicken by the end — uncover if too liquidy.
Dakdoritang is perfect cold-weather comfort food and reheats beautifully.

name: Jajangmyeon (Black Bean Noodles)
description: Thick wheat noodles smothered in a savory black bean paste sauce with diced pork and vegetables.
instructions: Dice pork, onion, zucchini, potato, and cabbage into 1 cm cubes.
Heat oil in a wok, fry chunjang paste for 2 minutes to remove raw taste.
Add diced pork and stir-fry until cooked through, about 3 minutes.
Add all vegetables and stir-fry 3 minutes, then add 300 ml water and sugar.
Simmer 10 minutes until vegetables are tender; thicken with cornstarch slurry.
Boil noodles, drain, and serve topped with the glossy black bean sauce; garnish with cucumber.
tips: Frying the chunjang paste first mellows its bitterness significantly.
Jajangmyeon is Korea's most popular delivery food — eaten on moving day tradition.

name: Kimbap (Korean Rice Rolls)
description: Seasoned rice and colorful fillings rolled in roasted seaweed, sliced into bite-sized rounds.
instructions: Cook rice and season with sesame oil and salt while still hot.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, season with sesame oil and salt.
Julienne carrot and saute briefly; make thin egg omelet strips.
Place seaweed sheet shiny-side down on a bamboo mat; spread rice thinly leaving 2 cm at the top edge.
Arrange fillings in a line across the center: spinach, carrot, egg, crab stick, and pickled radish.
Roll tightly using the mat, seal the edge with a grain of rice, brush with sesame oil, and slice into 1.5 cm rounds.
tips: Spread rice thinly — too much rice makes a heavy, unbalanced kimbap.
Brush the knife with sesame oil between cuts for clean slices.

name: Ojingeo Bokkeum (Spicy Squid Stir-Fry)
description: Tender squid stir-fried with vegetables in a fiery gochujang and gochugaru sauce.
instructions: Clean squid, score the body in a crosshatch pattern, and cut into pieces.
Slice onion, carrot, and cabbage; chop spring onion.
Mix gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, and minced garlic into a sauce.
Heat sesame oil in a wok over high heat and stir-fry squid for 2 minutes.
Add vegetables and sauce; stir-fry 3-4 minutes until vegetables are crisp-tender.
Finish with spring onion and sesame seeds; serve over steamed rice.
tips: Score the squid body so it curls attractively and absorbs more sauce.
Do not overcook squid — it becomes rubbery if stir-fried more than 3-4 minutes.

name: Yachae Jeon (Vegetable Pancake)
description: Crispy Korean vegetable pancake with zucchini, scallion, and chili in a light batter.
instructions: Julienne zucchini and carrot; cut spring onion into 5 cm lengths; slice chili.
Mix flour, cornstarch, salt, egg, and ice water into a thin batter.
Fold vegetables into the batter until just combined.
Heat oil generously in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat.
Spread vegetable batter in a thin, even layer and fry 3-4 minutes until bottom is golden and crisp.
Flip and fry 3 more minutes; serve cut into wedges with soy dipping sauce.
tips: A thin pancake fries crispier than a thick one — spread the batter wide.
Add a splash of soju to the batter for extra crispiness.

name: Kongbiji Jjigae (Ground Soybean Stew)
description: Thick, creamy stew of ground soybeans with kimchi and pork, a traditional Korean comfort dish.
instructions: Dice pork belly and chop kimchi into small pieces.
Heat sesame oil in a pot and saute pork belly until fat renders.
Add chopped kimchi, diced onion, and minced garlic; stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add ground soybean paste and water; stir well to combine.
Simmer on medium-low for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.
Season with gochugaru and salt; serve in stone bowls with spring onion.
tips: Fresh ground soybean paste can be made by blending soaked soybeans with water.
Stir frequently as the thick soybean base scorches easily on the bottom.

name: Budae Jjigae (Army Stew)
description: Spicy Korean hot pot with kimchi, spam, sausages, ramen noodles, and cheese in a gochugaru broth.
instructions: Slice spam and sausages into bite-sized pieces; cube tofu and chop kimchi.
Arrange all ingredients in sections in a wide shallow pot: kimchi, spam, sausages, tofu, baked beans.
Mix gochugaru, gochujang, and minced garlic into a paste; add to the center of the pot.
Pour anchovy-kelp stock over everything and bring to a rolling boil.
Once boiling, add ramen noodles and cook until softened, about 3 minutes.
Top with mozzarella cheese, cover briefly to melt, garnish with spring onion, and serve communally.
tips: Budae jjigae is a communal dish — serve in the center of the table for everyone to share.
This stew was created post-Korean War using surplus army base ingredients.

name: Bossam (Boiled Pork Wraps)
description: Tender boiled pork belly sliced thin, wrapped in napa cabbage with garlic, chili, and ssamjang.
instructions: Place whole pork belly in a large pot with halved onion, ginger, spring onion, doenjang, and instant coffee.
Cover with water, bring to a boil, then simmer for 60-70 minutes until pork is very tender.
Meanwhile, prepare cabbage by lightly salting leaves and letting them wilt for 20 minutes.
Remove pork from broth, let rest 10 minutes, then slice against the grain into 5 mm thick pieces.
Arrange pork slices on a platter with cabbage leaves, ssamjang, sliced garlic, and salted shrimp.
Wrap pork in cabbage leaves with desired condiments and eat in one bite.
tips: Instant coffee in the boiling liquid removes pork smell — a common Korean cooking trick.
Slice pork while warm for the cleanest cuts; cold pork crumbles.

name: Dwaeji Gukbap (Pork Rice Soup)
description: Milky pork bone broth soup with sliced pork and rice, a Busan specialty eaten with salted shrimp.
instructions: Blanch pork bones and shoulder in boiling water for 10 minutes to remove impurities; drain and rinse.
Place bones and shoulder in a fresh pot with water, spring onion, ginger, and garlic; boil vigorously for 90 minutes.
The vigorous boil emulsifies the fat into the broth, creating the milky white color.
Remove pork shoulder, slice thinly; strain broth and season with salt.
Place steamed rice in bowls, arrange sliced pork over rice.
Pour piping hot milky broth over, garnish with chives, and serve with salted shrimp and chili flakes on the side.
tips: The broth must boil vigorously (not simmer) to achieve the signature milky white color.
Dwaeji gukbap is Busan's most famous soul food — found at 24-hour restaurants citywide.

name: Sigeumchi Namul (Spinach Side)
description: Blanched spinach seasoned with sesame oil, garlic, and soy sauce, the most common Korean banchan.
instructions: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil.
Blanch spinach for 30-40 seconds until just wilted — do not overcook.
Immediately transfer to ice water to stop cooking and preserve bright green color.
Squeeze out as much water as possible from the spinach.
Toss with sesame oil, soy sauce, minced garlic, salt, and sesame seeds.
Serve at room temperature as a banchan side dish.
tips: Squeeze spinach very thoroughly — excess water dilutes the seasoning.
Sigeumchi namul keeps refrigerated for 3-4 days and is a staple in Korean lunch boxes.

name: Hobak Jeon (Zucchini Pancake)
description: Thin zucchini rounds dipped in egg and lightly pan-fried until golden, a delicate Korean side dish.
instructions: Slice zucchini into 5 mm thick rounds and lightly salt; let sit 5 minutes.
Pat zucchini slices dry with paper towels.
Dust each slice lightly with flour, then dip in beaten egg.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat.
Fry zucchini slices for 2 minutes per side until egg coating is golden.
Serve with a dipping sauce of soy sauce and rice vinegar.
tips: Salting the zucchini first draws out moisture for better frying.
These are simple enough for children to help make — a popular family cooking activity in Korea.

name: Dubu Jorim (Braised Tofu)
description: Firm tofu braised in a spicy, savory soy-gochugaru sauce, a popular Korean side dish.
instructions: Slice tofu into 1 cm thick rectangular pieces and pat dry.
Heat vegetable oil in a pan and pan-fry tofu slices for 2 minutes per side until lightly golden.
Mix soy sauce, gochugaru, minced garlic, sugar, sesame oil, and water into a braising sauce.
Pour sauce over the fried tofu in the pan.
Simmer on medium-low for 5-7 minutes, spooning sauce over the tofu occasionally.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and sesame seeds; serve as a side dish.
tips: Pan-frying first gives the tofu a firm exterior that holds up during braising.
Dubu jorim is one of the easiest and most common Korean banchan dishes.

name: Kimchi Bokkeumbap (Kimchi Fried Rice)
description: Wok-fried rice with aged kimchi, gochujang, and sesame oil, topped with a fried egg.
instructions: Chop kimchi into small pieces; reserve any kimchi juice.
Heat vegetable oil in a wok over high heat and stir-fry kimchi for 2-3 minutes until fragrant.
Add day-old rice, breaking up clumps, and stir-fry for 3 minutes.
Add gochujang, soy sauce, kimchi juice, and sesame oil; toss until rice is evenly coated and slightly crispy.
Fry eggs sunny-side up in a separate pan.
Plate rice, top with fried egg, spring onion, sesame seeds, and crumbled seaweed.
tips: Day-old refrigerated rice fries much better than freshly cooked rice.
The kimchi juice adds the sour depth that makes this dish irresistible.

name: Galbitang (Short Rib Soup)
description: Clear beef short rib soup simmered until the meat is tender, with glass noodles and radish.
instructions: Soak short ribs in cold water for 2 hours to remove blood; blanch in boiling water for 5 minutes and rinse.
Place ribs in a large pot with fresh water, garlic, ginger, and spring onion stems; bring to a boil.
Skim foam continuously for 10 minutes, then simmer on low for 90 minutes until meat is tender.
Add large chunks of daikon radish and cook 20 more minutes.
Cook glass noodles separately, drain, and divide into bowls.
Ladle soup and ribs over noodles; season with salt, pepper, and soy sauce; garnish with beaten egg drop and spring onion.
tips: Soaking the ribs is essential for a clear, clean-tasting broth.
Galbitang is a celebratory soup served at Korean weddings and holidays.
